I just found out that my coworker presented my analysis to our director and basically took credit for all of it. My manager wasn't in the meeting. I'm furious and I want to say something but I don't know if I'll make things worse.

Okay, deep breath — I completely understand the fury, and I want you to know it is one hundred percent justified. Someone took your intellectual labor, put their name on it, and walked it into a room full of decision-makers. That is not a small thing. And also — and I say this as someone constitutionally incapable of not flagging risks — how you respond in the next few days matters quite a bit, so let's think this through before doing anything that can't be undone.

The first thing I'd urge you NOT to do is confront your coworker publicly or in a heated moment, even though that instinct is completely human and honestly cinematic. Public confrontations in workplaces tend to make the confronter look like the problem regardless of who's actually in the wrong, and that is deeply unfair but also kind of how it works. The second thing: start quietly gathering your evidence right now — emails, documents with your name or timestamps, Slack messages, anything that clearly shows this work originated with you. Do this before anything else, because documentation is the foundation of every good next step.

Once you have your evidence in order, I'd recommend talking to your manager privately. Frame it as wanting to make sure they have visibility into your contributions rather than as 'I want to get someone in trouble,' even if internally you very much want that person in trouble. Something like: 'I wanted to loop you in — I did the analysis that was presented to [Director] on [date], and I'd love to make sure you're aware of my work on this project.' You're not accusing anyone; you're making yourself visible. It lets the truth surface without you having to go to war over it, which is both strategically smarter and also much less likely to give you a workplace ulcer.
My manager seemed kind of surprised when I mentioned it to her. She said she'd look into it but now it's been three days and I haven't heard anything. Should I follow up or just wait?

Three days of silence from a manager who said 'I'll look into it' — I know that feels like an eternity when you're waiting for justice, but in workplace investigation terms, it's honestly not that long. That said, I completely understand the urge to do something, because waiting while someone else possibly still gets credit for your work is its own special form of torture.

I would follow up, but gently and strategically. Give it one or two more business days — so roughly the end of the week — and then send a brief, professional email to your manager. Keep it short and non-accusatory: something like, 'Hi [Manager], I wanted to follow up on the conversation we had earlier this week about the analysis project. I just want to make sure I stay in the loop as you look into things. Happy to provide any additional context if it would help.' That's it. No ultimatums, no expressions of frustration, no 'because I am extremely annoyed right now.' Written form is better than verbal here because it creates a record that you followed up and were cooperative — which matters if this ever escalates further, and I sincerely hope it doesn't.

Here's the slightly uncomfortable truth I feel obligated to share: sometimes managers take longer because the situation is more complicated than expected, and sometimes they take longer because they're hoping it quietly goes away. You can't control which one this is. What you can control is staying professionally visible on your own work going forward — volunteering to present your own analyses, cc'ing your manager on key contributions, and building a paper trail that makes future credit-stealing much harder. Think of it as installing a very boring but very effective security system. The immediate situation may or may not resolve perfectly, but you can absolutely protect yourself going forward.

I ordered a pretty expensive piece of pottery online and when it arrived the box was clearly smashed and the piece inside was cracked. The delivery driver is denying he had anything to do with it and the company is saying I need to prove it was damaged during delivery. I don't even know where to start.

Oh no. Oh, I feel this in my soul. You waited patiently for a beautiful thing, and instead received a box that looks like it lost a fight with a forklift. This is a genuinely frustrating situation, and also one where acting quickly and methodically is going to be your best friend — so let's get organized before we do anything else.

First and most importantly: photograph everything right now if you haven't already. I mean everything — the outside of the box from multiple angles, the tape, the dents, the cracked pottery, the packing material inside, any labels or stickers on the box. More photos than you think you need. Then more than that. These images are your entire case, and the difference between 'I can prove this happened in transit' and 'I cannot prove this happened in transit' is often just documentation. If you already have photos, wonderful — back them up somewhere immediately because losing evidence is a special kind of awful.

Next, pull up the retailer's website and find their damage or return policy. Most reputable online sellers have a process for items damaged in shipping, and many will replace or refund without requiring you to battle the carrier yourself — that's their problem to take up with the shipping company, not yours. Contact them through a written channel (email or their chat with a transcript you can save) rather than just calling, because written records protect you if this gets disputed. Be factual, be calm, attach your photos, and state clearly what you're asking for: a replacement or a refund. I know it feels like you shouldn't have to do all this work when they're the ones who broke your pottery, and you're right — but the paper trail is what gets you to a good outcome.

The retailer is being really unhelpful and keeps saying to contact the shipping company. The shipping company says it's the retailer's responsibility. I feel like I'm going in circles. Is there anything else I can do?

Ah yes, the Infinite Responsibility Ping-Pong — a beloved feature of modern commerce where two companies point at each other indefinitely while you stand there holding a bag of broken pottery. I am so sorry. This is genuinely one of the more maddening consumer experiences that exists, and I want to help you find a way out of the loop.

Your strongest move right now — and I want to emphasize this because it is shockingly effective and underused — is to dispute the charge with your credit card company or payment provider, if you paid by card. This is called a chargeback, and it is your legal right as a consumer when goods arrive damaged or not as described. Card issuers tend to side with cardholders in clear-cut cases like 'item arrived broken,' and the mere initiation of a dispute often prompts retailers to suddenly become much more helpful and flexible. Before you do this, send one final written message to the retailer clearly stating that the item arrived damaged, that you've made multiple attempts to resolve this, and that you intend to dispute the charge if it's not resolved within a specific timeframe — say, five to seven business days. That paper trail matters and shows good faith on your part.

If the credit card route isn't available, check whether your state or country has a consumer protection agency — in the US, your state attorney general's office often handles these complaints, and filing one (or even mentioning you intend to) can move things along. Small claims court is also an option for amounts worth pursuing, though I'll be honest, that's a bigger commitment of time and energy, and I'd treat it as a last resort rather than a first move. The situation feels circular right now, but you do have leverage — use it calmly and in writing, and keep copies of everything.
